# Magic

If the combat system is the heart of Dominions 6, the magic system is the soul. Everything is
affected by magic in some way. The most powerful spells can summon awesome monsters or change
the face of the entire world. Magic can also be used to craft artifacts. Some basic points:

- Spells are either **rituals**, which are cast outside of combat, or **battle magic**, which
  is used in tactical battles.
- Magic depends both on the path and the school of magic.
- The path skill of a given mage is generally fixed and doesn't change easily.
- A school is a group of spells which can be learned through research, performed in
  laboratories.
- Magic gems and blood slaves are needed for many spells. Gems are produced by magic sites,
  including those in a nation's home province.
- Gems and blood slaves can be carried by commanders or stored in a nation's pool. Labs provide
  access to the national pool.
- The Construction school allows for the building of magical items and artifacts.
- There are also **Divine spells**, special battle magic spells that can be cast by priests.

## The Paths of Magical Power

There are nine paths of magical power. The first four are the elemental paths of **Fire, Air,
Water and Earth**. The next four are the sorcery paths of **Astral, Nature, Death and
Glamour**. Finally there is the path of **Blood**, which is part of neither elemental nor
sorcery magic.

There are eight types of magical gems corresponding to one path each. There are no Blood gems
-- Blood magic requires blood slaves, obtained through blood hunting.

A fire mage can eventually learn all of the Fire spells in all schools for which he has the
requisite magical ability, once his nation has researched them. But he cannot learn Nature or
Earth spells unless he first gains those path skills. While magic items can increase a mage's
skill in paths he already possesses, a mage can only acquire new paths through Empowerment.

## The Schools of Magical Research

There are seven schools of magical research: **Conjuration, Alteration, Evocation,
Construction, Enchantment, Thaumaturgy, and Blood Magic**. (The school of Blood Magic is not
the same as the path of Blood Magic, although the two are related.) To cast a spell, (a) your
nation must have researched the school up to the level required, (b) the mage must have
proficiency in the required path(s), and (c) the mage must have any gems or blood slaves
required.

**Conjuration**
: Summons powers and beings from other worlds. Many spells bring you additional units.

**Alteration**
: Changes the physical world (increased strength, fire resistance, weather changes, etc).

**Evocation**
: Projects arcane power into the physical world: fireballs, lightning bolts. Lots of battle
magic.

**Construction**
: Determines which magic items you can craft. Some construction spells make magic units.

**Enchantment**
: Grants magical properties to men, items or the land (protective domes, long-lasting health).

**Thaumaturgy**
: Manipulates the arcane world in ways traditionally associated with sorcery (sending souls
after a soldier, scrying distant lands).

**Blood Magic**
: All spells that use blood magic have to be unlocked by researching this school.

## Access to Magic Spells

Each spell has a path requirement, listed as a number of symbols. Spells that are a higher
level in a school don't necessarily require more path skill: Will o' the Wisp is a level 5
Conjuration spell but only requires Fire-1. Schools categorize spells but not rigorously
(Acashic Knowledge is a Conjuration spell that searches provinces for magic gems).

### Combined Paths

Some spells require knowledge of multiple paths. The mage must have the requisite level in each
path. When calculating bonuses for extra skill levels (see Fatigue), only the first magic path
listed is considered. Dual path spells and rituals use magic gems of the primary path.

### Empowerment

A mage may permanently increase his skill in any path by using Empowerment, which costs a lot
of magic gems. For the first skill level in a path, the cost is 50 magic gems of that type. For
subsequent increases the cost is 15 x [target level]. This is the only way to gain skill in a
path a unit doesn't have. Magic items that increase magic skill only work if the user's skill
in that path is at least 1 (as do battlefield spells like Power of the Spheres).

## Indirect Magic

Indirect magic represents the general arcane knowledge a mage possesses about any path in which
he has skill, giving side benefits. The more skill, the more benefit. The more powerful bonuses
usually have a minimum skill requirement of 3 or 4. At the first level usually only increased
leadership is gained, scaling with level.

| Path | Level 1+ | Level 3+ | Level 4+ |
|------|----------|----------|----------|
| Fire | Ldr +10/level, Magic Ldr +10/level, Shorter life | Fire Res +5 | Fire Res +5 |
| Air | Magic Ldr +10/level | Shock Res +5 | Shock Res +5 |
| Water | Magic Ldr +10/level | Cold Res +5 | Cold Res +5 |
| Earth | Magic Ldr +10/level | Natural Protection +3 | Affliction Resistance +1 |
| Astral | Magic Ldr +20/level | -- | Magic Resistance +1 |
| Death | Undead Ldr +50/level | Rarely dies of old age | Morale +10 |
| Nature | Magic Ldr +10/level, Supplies +10/level, Longer life | Poison Res +5 | Poison Res +5 |
| Glamour | Magic Ldr +10/level | False Damage Regen | True Sight |
| Blood | Undead Ldr +10/level, Magic Ldr +10/level | HP +5 | HP +5 |

: Indirect Magic bonuses

The False Damage Regen from glamour heals 1 point of false damage per combat round. Indirect
magic applies to all units with any magic skill, including pretenders.

> **Example:** Playing Asphodel, Carrion Woods, you select a Freak Lord pretender with Fire 2,
> Air 2, Water 3, Earth 4, and Blood 1. The pretender gains +20 Leadership and +20 Magic
> Leadership (Fire 2), +20 Magic Leadership (Air 2), +5 Cold Resistance and +30 Magic Leadership
> (Water 3), +3 Protection, +1 Affliction Resistance and +40 Magic Leadership (Earth 4), and +10
> Undead Leadership and +10 Magic Leadership (Blood 1).

## Types of spells

Spells are divided into two categories: Rituals and Battle Magic.

### Battle Magic

Battle magic can only be cast during a tactical battle. It can be scripted into a mage's list
of battle actions using Set battle orders. If a battle spell requires gems, that mage must have
those gems in his inventory before the battle begins. Some battle spells affect the whole
battlefield and are termed **battle enchantments**; a battle enchantment is dispelled if its
caster dies, and all battle enchantments and temporary effects are dispelled if the battle
lasts until sunset.

#### Interrupts

Spells must be prepared for a while before they can be cast; during this preparation the caster
can be interrupted if damaged. The preparation time is about half of the time required to cast
the spell (the other half is recovery). The chance of being interrupted is the percentage of
full hit points caused by the damage, plus 25%. A strike inflicting half a caster's full hit
points would have a 75% chance of interrupting.

Units with the "combat caster" ability (e.g. the paladin) and mindless units are half as
likely to be interrupted. Some monsters are innate spell casters that require no preparation
time.

#### Battle Magic mechanics

The method for resolving battle magic is very similar to that for missile weapons. Mages have a
Precision attribute, and spells have a Precision rating; for calculation these are added
together. Some spells (Acid Bolt) are armor-piercing (Protection counts for half); some (Orb
Lightning) are armor-negating (Protection bypassed entirely).

Some spells check the target's Magic Resistance instead of Protection. Magic Resistance is most
often binary: it either protects or it does not. Some spells (mostly Astral) require the caster
to defeat the target's Magic Resistance:

- **Caster's Penetration roll:** 11 + DRN + (additional skill in spell path) / 2
- **Target's MR roll:** Magic Resistance + DRN + (skill in spell path) / 2

The caster wins ties. "Magic resistance negates easily" gives the caster a -4 penetration
modifier ("easy to resist"); "hard to resist" gives +4.

#### Fatigue

Casting spells in battle costs fatigue. For each skill level in the required path the mage
exceeds the minimum, he incurs 1 / (1 + (mage skill - minimum skill)) of the listed spell
fatigue. So one extra skill level means half fatigue, two means a third, three means a quarter,
and so on.

Spell casters also incur fatigue equal to their base Encumbrance + 2x the Encumbrance of any
armor worn for each spell cast (not subject to skill reduction). Fatigue is very important for
spell casters; higher-skill mages have an advantage. Spells like Reinvigoration or Relief can
be very useful.

### Rituals

Rituals are spells with an effect on the world map, taking an entire month (game turn) to cast.
If a ritual requires gems, the mage may only use gems from a laboratory, and rituals can only
be cast in provinces with labs. Some rituals affect the entire province they are cast in;
some of these (called **local enchantments**) last longer than one turn. An example is Dome of
Air, which persists until dispelled (or until the caster dies, or usually until the province is
conquered). Some local enchantments have a limited duration that can be extended by adding
extra gems during creation (usually 1 month per extra gem, some up to 3 months per gem).

Rituals can be automated to recast each month by the same mage using `Shift+m` when selected
in a laboratory province. If a spell didn't get cast, the most likely problem is insufficient
gems of the correct type.

## Global Enchantments

Global enchantments are very powerful rituals that affect the entire world. Only a certain
number (3, 5, 7, or 9, set in Game Setup) can be in effect at once (view with `F6`). When
casting one, you can add more gems than required, because the more gems used, the harder it is
to dispel. The maximum number of gems a mage can use to cast a ritual is his path level x 100.

Like local enchantments, globals are dependent on their caster: if the caster dies, the global
is dispelled (extra gems don't protect against assassination). Some globals are tied to a
specific province (e.g. Stellar Focus); if that country is conquered, the global is dispelled.

### Dispelling global enchantments

Globals can be dispelled by casting a dispel ritual (the common one is **Dispel**, an
Enchantment-5 spell requiring Astral-3), or by casting another global when the maximum is
already active (replacing a random existing one). The exception: if you cast a global already
in effect by another nation, your version tries to replace the enemy version. In order:

- If there is already a global of the same name active, the new global attempts to replace it.
- If there are fewer than the max active and not already an instance of this spell, the new
  global immediately fills an empty slot.
- If the maximum is already active, the new global attempts to replace a randomly chosen spell
  (which can be one of your own).

#### Dispel mechanics

When Dispel is cast or a global tries to replace another, the two spells are compared:

- +1 for each extra gem (above the casting requirement)
- +5 for each level of skill by the casting mage in the spell's path above the requirement

A DRN is added to each side; the higher number wins.

#### Protection vs global enchantments

Most globals that affect units can be protected against with Enchanted Domes (e.g. Dome of
Solid Air), except strikeback (trap) domes. Many global enchantments only take effect inside
their own dominion; protecting against these can be done by pushing away the enemy dominion.

### Grand Communions

Some nations have perfected the communal casting of rituals. When a mage with the Grand
Communion ability casts a global ritual, other mages with the same ability can use the 'Grand
Communion' order to add their skill level to the power of the ritual.

> **Example:** An Arch Theurg casts dispel with 12 extra gems. Two Theurgs (astral 2 each) and
> four Theurg Acolytes (astral 1 each) join via Grand Communion, adding 4 + 4 = 8, bringing the
> dispel attempt's strength to 20.

## Communions

The Astral and Blood paths contain a special spell type called **communion** that lets mages
temporarily increase their magic skill in combat and distribute spellcasting fatigue among
other friendly mages. It requires two mages: one casting the master spell, one the slave spell.
The Astral spells are **Communion Master** and **Communion Slave**; the Blood equivalents are
**Sabbath Master** and **Sabbath Slave** (which cost blood slaves).

**Level bonus**
: A communion master gains *n* extra levels in each of his known magic paths for every 2^n
slaves. So a master with 2 slaves gains +1, with 4 slaves gains +2.

**Fatigue distribution**
: The fatigue assigned to each participant is the cost of the master's spell divided by the
number of participants, modified by slave skill:
  - slave level = master level: no modification
  - slave level > master level: fatigue / 2
  - slave level < master level / 2: fatigue x 4
  - slave level < master level: fatigue x 2

A participant is the communion master casting the spell plus all friendly communion slaves on
the battlefield. Slaves cannot act on their own but benefit from self-buffs cast by the
master. If the communion breaks because all masters are dead or fled, slaves suffer a backlash:
stunned for about one round and 3d50 fatigue damage each. Some magic items (Crystal Matrix,
Slave Matrix, Slave's Heart, Master's Athame) let mages participate automatically without
casting the spell.

**Communal Chants:** Spellsingers of MA Man have Chorus Master and Chorus Slave (Chorus Slaves
leave the communion when knocked unconscious).

There can be one communion of each type (communion / sabbath / chorus) active at once, with
separate masters, slaves, and bonuses:

- **Communion:** 25% longer casting time for spells.
- **Sabbath:** only half as much fatigue on the caster, but 20% extra fatigue on slaves.
- **Chorus:** 25% longer casting time. Slaves drop out when losing consciousness.

## Magic Gems

Gems are the physical manifestation of the paths of arcane power. They are generated by magic
sites and added automatically to the magic resource treasury (`F7`) each turn, as long as
there is a connection through friendly territory to a province with a laboratory.

The magic resource treasury lists all your gems and your acquisition rate. The "pool" button
collects all gems from commanders stationed in a province with a lab (useful for blood
hunters). You can also transfer gems between commanders.

### Using magic gems in combat

Mages can use magic gems in combat to temporarily raise their skill level and to reduce
fatigue. A mage can only use as many gems as his current skill level in that path each turn.
Using a magic gem gains one skill level in that path (to cast a spell beyond his skill, reduce
fatigue, or both), but a mage may never increase his skill by more than one via gems, nor gain
skill in a path in which he had none.

You may script spells the mage lacks skill for and place the necessary gems in his inventory,
or include extra gems for the computer to use to reduce fatigue. Checking "conservative gem
use" makes the mage use gems sparingly and for scripted spells only. Some spells increase a
caster's skill for the whole combat duration (unlike gems, which last only one spell in one
round).

## Research

The research bars in the Arcane Laboratory (`F5`) show research progress per school. Research
speed equals the total research abilities of all commanders ordered to research that turn, who
must be in a province with a friendly laboratory. Normally only commanders with magic skill can
research; the higher a mage's magic skills, the more research points.

Research points are affected by Magic/Drain scales. A few nations have special researchers
without magic skill: philosophers of Arcoscephale and Therodoros, and units with Divine
Insights (Monks of Man, Cloistered Ladies of Feminie). Divinely-inspired researchers in a lab
are capped at the dominion strength in the province. Philosophers get a research bonus from
Sloth scales and are unaffected by Magic or Drain scales.

- **Research ability:** (5 + (2x magic levels of mage)) +/- research bonus/penalty (never below
  1).
- The affliction Dementia reduces research ability by half.

### Legendary Spells

Level 9 of all magic schools except construction consists of legendary spells. When you
research this level you choose a single spell. The others can be researched too, but one at a
time, making it very expensive to get all the level 9 spells of a school. They are usually very
powerful or more cost-effective than lesser ones.

## Magic Items

The Construction school allows you to construct magic items by giving the order "Forge Item" to
a mage in a laboratory. The items at research level 9 are **artifacts** and are unique (only
one of each can exist). Other items can be forged without restriction, given the appropriate
Construction research level, skill, and gems.

Unique artifacts start to **yearn** to be forged once their creation seems within reach; a
yearning artifact can be forged at half the usual cost. An artifact may start yearning once at
least one of these has occurred (each increases the yearning rate by 50%):

- At least one nation has researched Construction level 9.
- The global enchantment Forge of the Ancients has been cast.
- The Throne of Creation has been claimed.
- The Throne of the Artificer has been claimed.

A mage can be set to repeatedly forge the same item every turn with `Shift+o`. Some items are
restricted to certain nations (e.g. Black Halberd for MA Ulm) and only show up in the forge for
those nations (with a dark blue background). Items discounted for certain nations (e.g.
Blacksteel Sword for MA Ulm) are shown with a gray background.

## Divine Magic

Divine spells are a special category that does not depend on research or magic paths. Instead,
they depend on the **Holy** skill of the caster, available to priests or other units with at
least one level of Holy skill. All common divine spells are available to all nations at the
start of the game. All divine magic consists of battlefield spells.

Some divine spells depend on the magic skills of the pretender god. The spells **Banishment**
and **Smite** have alternative versions that replace the originals if the pretender is
particularly skilled (level 4 or higher) in one magic path. The highest magic skill of the
pretender determines the replacement; in case of a tie, the order below decides. If a
pretender's magic does not reach the threshold in any path, priests retain Banishment and Smite
as normal.

> **Example:** The priests of a pretender with 6 Air, 4 Earth, and 6 Death would know Wind of
> Memories and Heavenly Strike instead of Banishment and Smite.

| Path | Smite replacement | Special Effect |
|------|-------------------|----------------|
| Fire | Heavenly Fire | 10 armor-negating fire damage is a secondary effect. |
| Air | Heavenly Strike | Range 50. 5 armor-negating shock damage. |
| Water | Watery Death | Slightly shorter range. 10 armor-negating drowning damage is a secondary effect. |
| Earth | Word of Stone | Slightly shorter range. Petrification is a secondary effect. |
| Astral | Word of Power | Range 100. Secondary effect can paralyze non-mindless units. |
| Death | Syllable of Death | Slightly shorter range. Secondary effect can kill living beings or exhaust them. |
| Nature | Word of Thorns | Reduces damage. Entanglement and bleeding as secondary effect. |
| Glamour | Word of Bewilderment | Confusion as secondary effect. |
| Blood | Claim Life | Can only target living beings. Increased damage. Gives Chest Wound affliction as a secondary effect. |

: Divine spells replacing Smite

| Path | Banishment replacement | Special Effect |
|------|------------------------|----------------|
| Fire | Ashes to Ashes | Causes burning as a secondary effect. |
| Air | Wind of Memories | Increased range, much increased area of effect, lower damage. |
| Water | Purifying Water | Increased area of effect, slightly lower damage. Secondary effect causes additional armor-piercing damage (mostly harms unarmored targets). |
| Earth | Pull from the Grave | Causes Earth Grip as a secondary effect. |
| Astral | Stellar Decree | Increase range and area of effect, but slightly lower damage. Secondary effect stuns undead with their minds intact. |
| Death | Decree of the Underworld | Secondary effect causes bewilderment of undead with their minds intact. |
| Nature | Final Rest | Reduced area of effect, easily resisted but kills targets. |
| Glamour | Return of the Past | Increased range and area of effect, slightly lower damage. Extra secondary damage that only affects non-mindless undead. |
| Blood | (none) | There is no Blood Magic spell that replaces Banishment. |

: Divine spells replacing Banishment

## Alchemy

Magic gems can be converted from one type to another through alchemy, by any commander with
magical skill (in a province with a friendly laboratory) using the "Alchemy" order. In general,
alchemy can only convert gems into Astral gems, or convert Astral gems into gems of any other
type, at a rate of 2:1 (old:new). Converting to Astral and then to another type in a single
turn is effectively 4:1, so it gets expensive.

Alchemy is also possible through magic: ritual Earth and Fire spells of the Alteration school
convert gems to gold (rather than gems to pearls).
